dependencies.description: MP-BGP with VPNv4/VPNv6 address families is required for MPLS L3VPN inter-VRF route distribution and route-target based import/export.  VRF-Lite does not require BGP.
dependencies.name: BGP (for MPLS VPN)

dependencies.description: MPLS label switching is required for L3VPN transport between PE routers.  LDP or Segment Routing provides label distribution.  VRF-Lite does not require MPLS.
dependencies.name: MPLS (for label distribution)

dependencies.description: IP routing must be enabled on the device.  Each VRF maintains its own independent routing table, RIB, and FIB.
dependencies.name: IP routing

failure_modes.description: An interface that should be in a VRF is either not assigned to any VRF (so it participates in the global routing table) or is assigned to the wrong VRF.  Traffic that should be isolated within the VRF is instead forwarded using the global RIB/FIB, breaking tenant isolation and causing reachability failures within the VRF.  On IOS-XE and NX-OS, adding or changing the VRF membership of an interface silently removes its IP address — if the address is not re-applied, the interface comes up with no L3 configuration.

resolutions.action: Assign the interface to the correct VRF: 'vrf member <name>' (NX-OS) or 'vrf forwarding <name>' (IOS-XE)
Assign the interface to the correct VRF: 'vrf member <name>' (NX-OS) or 'vrf forwarding <name>' (IOS-XE)
Assign the interface to the correct VRF: 'vrf member <name>' (NX-OS) or 'vrf forwarding <name>' (IOS-XE)
Immediately re-apply the IP address after VRF assignment — it WILL be removed
Immediately re-apply the IP address after VRF assignment — it WILL be removed
Immediately re-apply the IP address after VRF assignment — it WILL be removed
Verify with 'show vrf interface' that the interface appears under the correct VRF
Verify with 'show vrf interface' that the interface appears under the correct VRF
Verify with 'show vrf interface' that the interface appears under the correct VRF
On IOS-XR, VRF assignment does NOT remove the IP — but still verify with 'show vrf <name>'
On IOS-XR, VRF assignment does NOT remove the IP — but still verify with 'show vrf <name>'
On IOS-XR, VRF assignment does NOT remove the IP — but still verify with 'show vrf <name>'

root_causes.cause: 'vrf member' (NX-OS) or 'vrf forwarding' (IOS-XE) not configured on the interface
VRF assignment removed the IP address (IOS-XE/NX-OS behavior) and IP was not re-configured
Interface was bounced or reloaded without re-applying VRF + IP configuration
Wrong VRF name configured on the interface (typo or stale config)

failure_modes.scenario: Interface in wrong VRF — traffic routed in global table instead of VRF
symptoms: Ping fails across VRF — destinations within the VRF are unreachable
Routes not appearing in the expected VRF routing table
'show ip route vrf <name>' missing directly connected networks
Interface has no IP address after VRF assignment (IOS-XE/NX-OS)
Traffic leaking into the global table instead of staying in the VRF

failure_modes.description: Inter-VRF route leaking is configured but routes are not being imported from one VRF into another.  This typically relies on MP-BGP with VPNv4/VPNv6 address families and route-target (RT) import/export policies.  When misconfigured, routes remain isolated in their originating VRF and cannot be reached from other VRFs, breaking shared-services or inter-tenant communication.

resolutions.action: Verify RT alignment: exporting VRF's 'route-target export X:Y' must match importing VRF's 'route-target import X:Y'
Verify RT alignment: exporting VRF's 'route-target export X:Y' must match importing VRF's 'route-target import X:Y'
Verify RT alignment: exporting VRF's 'route-target export X:Y' must match importing VRF's 'route-target import X:Y'
Configure MP-BGP address-family vpnv4/vpnv6 unicast for each VRF that participates in route leaking
Configure MP-BGP address-family vpnv4/vpnv6 unicast for each VRF that participates in route leaking
Configure MP-BGP address-family vpnv4/vpnv6 unicast for each VRF that participates in route leaking
Redistribute connected/static routes into BGP under the VRF address-family if needed
Redistribute connected/static routes into BGP under the VRF address-family if needed
Redistribute connected/static routes into BGP under the VRF address-family if needed
Check route-map filters on import/export — temporarily remove to isolate the issue
Check route-map filters on import/export — temporarily remove to isolate the issue
Check route-map filters on import/export — temporarily remove to isolate the issue
For VRF-Lite without BGP: use static routes with next-hop in the other VRF via a shared interface
For VRF-Lite without BGP: use static routes with next-hop in the other VRF via a shared interface
For VRF-Lite without BGP: use static routes with next-hop in the other VRF via a shared interface

root_causes.cause: Missing 'import' or 'export' route-target configuration on one or both VRFs
RT values do not match between exporting VRF's export RT and importing VRF's import RT
MP-BGP address-family vpnv4/vpnv6 not configured for the VRF under the BGP process
Route-map on the import/export direction filtering out the desired routes
BGP not redistributing the connected/static routes into the VRF address-family

failure_modes.scenario: Route leaking between VRFs not working — inter-VRF routing fails
symptoms: Cannot reach routes in other VRF even though route leaking is configured
BGP VPNv4/VPNv6 routes not being imported into target VRF
'show bgp vpnv4 unicast vrf <name>' shows no imported routes
Shared services (DNS, NTP) unreachable from tenant VRFs

failure_modes.description: Network services such as DHCP relay, NTP, SNMP, SSH, syslog, or TACACS+ are not reachable from within a VRF because the service is not bound to the VRF context.  Many services default to the global routing table and require explicit VRF-aware configuration.  This is a common issue in management VRF deployments where the management interface is in a VRF but services are configured globally.

resolutions.action: NX-OS: configure 'use-vrf management' under each service (ntp, snmp-server, etc.)
NX-OS: configure 'use-vrf management' under each service (ntp, snmp-server, etc.)
NX-OS: configure 'use-vrf management' under each service (ntp, snmp-server, etc.)
IOS-XE: configure 'ip ssh source-interface', 'ip tacacs source-interface', etc., using an interface in the correct VRF
IOS-XE: configure 'ip ssh source-interface', 'ip tacacs source-interface', etc., using an interface in the correct VRF
IOS-XE: configure 'ip ssh source-interface', 'ip tacacs source-interface', etc., using an interface in the correct VRF
Verify routing in the VRF can reach the service endpoint: 'show ip route vrf <name> <server-ip>'
Verify routing in the VRF can reach the service endpoint: 'show ip route vrf <name> <server-ip>'
Verify routing in the VRF can reach the service endpoint: 'show ip route vrf <name> <server-ip>'
Check platform documentation for VRF-aware service support — not all services support all VRFs on all platforms
Check platform documentation for VRF-aware service support — not all services support all VRFs on all platforms
Check platform documentation for VRF-aware service support — not all services support all VRFs on all platforms

root_causes.cause: Service not bound to the VRF — missing 'use-vrf' (NX-OS) or 'vrf forwarding' (IOS-XE) under the service config
Source interface for the service is in a different VRF than the service binding
No route in the VRF to reach the service endpoint (server IP)
ACL or firewall blocking traffic in the VRF context
Service does not support VRF-awareness on the platform/version

failure_modes.scenario: VRF-aware service misconfiguration — management services not working in VRF
symptoms: Management services (SSH, SNMP, NTP) unreachable from VRF
DHCP relay not forwarding requests from VRF interfaces
SSH sessions fail when connecting via management VRF
SNMP polling times out for devices using management VRF
Syslog messages not reaching the server from VRF context

verify_commands.look_for: Check which services are bound to which VRF — look for 'use-vrf', 'vrf forwarding', 'vrf member' under service configs

verify_commands.confirms_if: Service is bound to the correct VRF in the running config
verify_commands.look_for: Check which services are bound to which VRF — look for 'use-vrf', 'vrf forwarding', 'vrf member' under service configs

failure_modes.description: Route Distinguisher (RD) values are reused across VRFs that should be distinct, or Route Target (RT) import policies are too broad, causing routes from unrelated VRFs to leak into each other.  RDs make VPNv4 prefixes globally unique in BGP — if two VRFs on different PEs use the same RD for the same prefix, BGP may suppress one as a duplicate.  Overly broad RT imports pull in routes from VRFs that should be isolated.

resolutions.action: Use unique RD per VRF per PE — common convention: <loopback-ip>:<VRF-id> ensures global uniqueness
Use unique RD per VRF per PE — common convention: <loopback-ip>:<VRF-id> ensures global uniqueness
Use unique RD per VRF per PE — common convention: <loopback-ip>:<VRF-id> ensures global uniqueness
Audit RT import/export policies — each VRF should only import RTs it explicitly needs
Audit RT import/export policies — each VRF should only import RTs it explicitly needs
Audit RT import/export policies — each VRF should only import RTs it explicitly needs
If using auto-RD/auto-RT, verify the generated values are unique across all PEs
If using auto-RD/auto-RT, verify the generated values are unique across all PEs
If using auto-RD/auto-RT, verify the generated values are unique across all PEs
For EVPN: prefer Type-1 RD (IP:nn) over Type-0 (ASN:nn) for guaranteed uniqueness
For EVPN: prefer Type-1 RD (IP:nn) over Type-0 (ASN:nn) for guaranteed uniqueness
For EVPN: prefer Type-1 RD (IP:nn) over Type-0 (ASN:nn) for guaranteed uniqueness

root_causes.cause: Same RD used for different VRFs on different PEs — BGP treats same RD+prefix as same route and may suppress one
RT import configured too broadly (e.g., importing a wildcard or shared RT that multiple VRFs export)
Auto-RT feature generating conflicting RT values across VRFs
Copy-paste error during VRF provisioning — RD/RT from template not updated

failure_modes.scenario: RD/RT collision — overlapping route-distinguishers cause route confusion
symptoms: Wrong routes appearing in a VRF — routes from another customer/tenant visible
Routes from other VRFs leaking unexpectedly
BGP bestpath selecting incorrect VPNv4 route due to duplicate RD+prefix
Route suppression — valid routes not installed because BGP sees them as duplicates

verify_commands.look_for: Check which prefixes are associated with this RD — if routes from multiple unrelated VRFs appear, the RD is reused

verify_commands.look_for: Check RD and RT import/export values for all VRFs — look for duplicate RDs or overly broad RT imports

failure_modes.description: The VRF routing table or hardware FIB partition has reached its capacity limit.  New routes cannot be installed in the VRF, causing traffic to be dropped for destinations whose routes were not programmed.  This can happen when a full BGP internet table is imported into a VRF, when too many VRFs share a limited TCAM partition, or when the platform's per-VRF scale limit is exceeded.

resolutions.action: Apply prefix-list or route-map to limit routes imported into the VRF
Apply prefix-list or route-map to limit routes imported into the VRF
Apply prefix-list or route-map to limit routes imported into the VRF
Use route summarization to reduce the number of prefixes in the VRF
Use route summarization to reduce the number of prefixes in the VRF
Use route summarization to reduce the number of prefixes in the VRF
Adjust TCAM/SDM template to allocate more space for VRF routes (requires reload on some platforms)
Adjust TCAM/SDM template to allocate more space for VRF routes (requires reload on some platforms)
Adjust TCAM/SDM template to allocate more space for VRF routes (requires reload on some platforms)
Use default route in the VRF instead of importing the full table
Use default route in the VRF instead of importing the full table
Use default route in the VRF instead of importing the full table
Check platform scale limits: 'get_scale_limits' for VRF and FIB capacity
Check platform scale limits: 'get_scale_limits' for VRF and FIB capacity
Check platform scale limits: 'get_scale_limits' for VRF and FIB capacity

root_causes.cause: Full BGP internet table (1M+ routes) imported into a VRF that has limited TCAM
TCAM partition for VRFs too small — default allocation may not be sufficient
Too many VRFs configured, each consuming a share of the limited hardware forwarding resources
Route summarization not used — thousands of specific routes consuming TCAM unnecessarily
Leak of routes from global table into VRF via misconfigured RT import

failure_modes.scenario: VRF route table full — VRF-specific FIB exhaustion
symptoms: New routes not installed in the VRF — syslog shows FIB or TCAM errors
'show ip route vrf <name> summary' shows fewer routes than expected
Traffic to certain destinations blackholed while others work fine
BGP peers in the VRF showing routes in RIB but not in FIB (RIB-failure or similar)
Platform syslog: ADJMGR, FIB, or TCAM exhaustion messages

verify_commands.look_for: Check total route count vs platform limit — if near or at the limit, FIB exhaustion is confirmed

verify_commands.look_for: Check total route count vs platform limit — if near or at the limit, FIB exhaustion is confirmed

verify_commands.confirms_if: Route count at or near platform FIB limit for VRF
verify_commands.look_for: Check total route count vs platform limit — if near or at the limit, FIB exhaustion is confirmed

verify_commands.look_for: Check TCAM/FIB utilization — look for entries near 100% or 'full' indicators in the VRF partition

purpose: Virtual routing instance that partitions a single physical router into multiple independent routing domains, providing L3 traffic isolation for multi-tenant and VPN deployments

config_examples:
vrf definition TENANT-A
 rd 10.0.0.1:100
 address-family ipv4
  route-target import 65000:100
  route-target export 65000:100
 exit-address-family
!
interface GigabitEthernet1/0/1
 vrf forwarding TENANT-A
 ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown

notes.note: 'vrf definition <name>' (new-style) or 'ip vrf <name>' (legacy).  New-style requires 'address-family ipv4' / 'address-family ipv6' under the VRF definition.  Adding 'vrf forwarding <name>' to an interface REMOVES the IP address — MUST re-configure IP immediately.  'ip route vrf <name>' for per-VRF static routes.

config_examples:
vrf TENANT-A
 address-family ipv4 unicast
  import route-target
   65000:100
  export route-target
   65000:100
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/1
 vrf TENANT-A
 ipv4 address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0
 no shutdown

notes.note: 'vrf <name>' under global config.  RD and RT configured under 'vrf <name> address-family ipv4/ipv6 unicast'.  Interface assignment: 'vrf <name>' under interface config.  IOS-XR does NOT remove the IP address when assigning a VRF — most VRF-aware platform for services.

config_examples:
vrf context TENANT-A
  rd 10.0.0.1:100
  address-family ipv4 unicast
    route-target import 65000:100
    route-target export 65000:100
!
interface Ethernet1/1
  vrf member TENANT-A
  ip address 10.1.1.1/24
  no shutdown

notes.note: 'vrf context <name>' to create the VRF.  'vrf member <name>' under interface to assign it — this REMOVES the IP address and it must be re-added.  RD and RT configured under 'vrf context'.  'feature bgp' + address-family vpnv4/vpnv6 unicast required for MPLS VPN.  VRF-Lite does not require BGP or MPLS.

config_examples:
# Create VRF
config vrf add Vrf_TENANT-A
# Bind interface
config interface vrf bind Ethernet0 Vrf_TENANT-A
config interface ip add Ethernet0 10.1.1.1/24
# Verify
show vrf
ip vrf exec Vrf_TENANT-A ping 10.1.1.2

notes.note: VRF via CONFIG_DB VRF table.  'config vrf add <name>' / 'config vrf del <name>' to manage VRFs.  'ip vrf exec <name> <cmd>' to run commands in VRF context.  Management VRF is common.  Limited VRF support compared to Cisco IOS-XE/IOS-XR — no MPLS VPN, no RT-based route leaking.  VRF-aware static routes and BGP supported.
